coredata.dc:title: Controllable preparation of solid-state fluorescent polymer dots by gaseous detonation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Saiwei
Xu, Weikai
He, Chuang
Zhou, Shengbo
Yan, Honghao
coredata.dc:description: Polymer dots (PDs) have attracted significant research interest owing to their remarkable optical properties. However, high-performance PDs often suffer from severe aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching in the solid state, limiting their practical applications. The gaseous detonation method offers a promising approach for synthesizing PDs while overcoming this limitation. Despite its potential, the relationship between detonation reaction conditions and the morphology and optical properties of PDs remains largely unexplored. This study systematically investigates the effects of varying the total precursor mass (citric acid (CA) and urea), the molar ratio of H2 to O2, and the mass ratio of CA to urea on the morphology and optical properties of PDs. The results indicate that the initial reaction conditions, including the total mass of precursors, the molar ratio of H2 to O2, and the mass ratio of CA to urea, affect the microstructure (average particle size, crystallinity) and photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of the prepared PDs. However, it was found that these conditions have almost no effect on the ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectra, photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra, and the types of surface groups of the products. This study provides a theoretical foundation for optimizing the preparation of PDs using the gaseous detonation method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer dots
Detonation reaction conditions
Morphology
Optical properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of P1-P9 at different magnifications (with the size distribution, the crystal lattice of single PD, and the FFT patterns).
The influence of different factor levels on PDs average particle size.
UV–Vis spectra of (a) P1-P4 and (b) P5-P9.
The optical photographs of P1-P9 under visible light and 365 nm UV light (from left to right, P1-P9): (a) aqueous solutions and (b) solid products.
PL emission spectra of P1-P9 aqueous solution.
PL emission spectra of P1-P9 solid-state PDs.
(a) PLE and (b) PL emission spectra of P9 aqueous solutions with different concentrations.
The influence of different factor levels on PLQY.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: The Arabic numerals in parentheses indicate the level values.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM analyses of PDs synthesized by gaseous detonation.
Range and variance analysis of mean particle size of PDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: If FA > F0.01, the factor is highly significant and denoted as **; if F0.01 > FA > F0.05, the factor is significant and denoted as *; if F0.05 > FA > F0.1, the factor has an effect and is denoted as ⊙; if F0.1 > FA > F0.2, the factor has a certain effect and is denoted as △; if FA > F0.2, the factor has no effect. The same applies below.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Range and variance analysis of PLQY of PDs.

head.ce:title: Controllable preparation of solid-state fluorescent polymer dots by gaseous detonation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanics and Aerospace Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanics and Aerospace Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanics and Aerospace Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymer dots (PDs) have attracted significant research interest owing to their remarkable optical properties. However, high-performance PDs often suffer from severe aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching in the solid state, limiting their practical applications. The gaseous detonation method offers a promising approach for synthesizing PDs while overcoming this limitation. Despite its potential, the relationship between detonation reaction conditions and the morphology and optical properties of PDs remains largely unexplored. This study systematically investigates the effects of varying the total precursor mass (citric acid (CA) and urea), the molar ratio of H2 to O2, and the mass ratio of CA to urea on the morphology and optical properties of PDs. The results indicate that the initial reaction conditions, including the total mass of precursors, the molar ratio of H2 to O2, and the mass ratio of CA to urea, affect the microstructure (average particle size, crystallinity) and photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of the prepared PDs. However, it was found that these conditions have almost no effect on the ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectra, photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra, and the types of surface groups of the products. This study provides a theoretical foundation for optimizing the preparation of PDs using the gaseous detonation method.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors confirm that the data supporting the findings of this study are available within the article. Any additional data or information can be obtained from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of carbon-based zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterials with good biocompatibility, low toxicity, unique fluorescence tunability, excellent catalytic properties, and outstanding photostability [1–5], are widely used in biomedicine, optoelectronic devices, catalysis, and chemical sensing [6–11]. Based on their structures, CDs are classified into three categories: graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and polymer dots (PDs) [12]. However, PDs in the solid state undergo the fluorescence quenching effect, also known as the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching effect. Specifically, this effect refers to the phenomenon in which the fluorescence of PDs diminishes or even disappears at high concentrations or in the solid state [13,14]. This greatly limits the application of PDs in the field of optical devices.

To overcome the fluorescence quenching effect of PDs in the solid state, the existing means are mainly encapsulation methods and the design of new structures or emission centers. For the encapsulation method, PDs are embedded in a solid matrix such as starch [15], silica gel [16], polymer [17], and trisodium citrate [18], so that the distance between neighboring PDs is larger than their interaction distance, thus achieving solid-state fluorescence. However, this method cannot fundamentally overcome the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching effect of PDs, and the solid matrix only acts as a dispersant like water. Meanwhile, most of the encapsulation methods are unable to synthesize solid-state fluorescent PDs in situ, and a two-step method is required. That is, PDs are prepared first and then doped into the solid substrate, which is time-consuming and complicated to operate. For designing new structures and emission centers, this method allows the one-step preparation of solid-state fluorescent PDs by controlling the particle spacing or attenuating the surface interactions of PDs, fundamentally overcoming the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching effect. However, this technology mainly uses the microwave method and hydrothermal/solvothermal method to prepare solid-state fluorescent PDs [19–21], which is not suitable for large-scale preparation of solid-state fluorescent PDs. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a fast and controllable method for the large-scale preparation of solid-state fluorescent PDs.

As a new method for preparing nanomaterials, although the gaseous detonation method has disadvantages such as complex equipment and low yield, it has the advantages of fast preparation speed, strong controllability, high repeatability, and simple operation. Therefore, it is expected to become a potential method for preparing PDs. To be more specific, the gaseous detonation method uses combustible gas and O2 as the source of detonation, which generates a detonation wave acting on the reactants after ignition, and directly synthesizes nanomaterials by a series of chemical reactions within milliseconds [22]. Currently, nanomaterials such as titanium dioxide [23], carbon nano-onions [24], carbon nanotubes [25], and carbon-coated metals [26,27] have been successfully synthesized using the gaseous detonation method. In addition, our group successfully prepared solid-state fluorescent CDs and GQDs by the gaseous detonation method [28,29]. However, as a type of CDs, the preparation of solid-state fluorescent PDs by the gaseous detonation method has not been reported. More importantly, the relationship between the detonation reaction conditions and the optical properties of solid-state fluorescent PDs has not been clarified. This is essential for better control of the synthesis of solid-state fluorescent PDs.

This study designs an orthogonal experiment that uses three initial reaction conditions as factors: the total mass of precursors (citric acid (CA) and urea), the molar ratio of H2 to O2, and the mass ratio of CA to urea. Each factor is set at three levels to synthesize a series of PDs with varying properties. The prepared PDs are characterized using techniques such as Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Fourier-Transform Infrared (FT-IR) Spectroscopy, Ultraviolet-Visible Spectroscopy (UV–Vis), and Photoluminescence (PL) spectra. The effects of different initial reaction conditions on the average particle size, crystallinity, and photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of PDs are examined. This study aims to identify the optimal conditions for preparing PDs via the gaseous detonation method, providing a theoretical and technical foundation for the production of PDs.

CA (AR grade) and urea (AR grade) were provided by Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). H2 and O2 with a purity of 99.999 % were provided by Dalian Special Gases Co., Ltd. (China). All chemicals were utilized in their received condition without additional purification.

The preparation method for PDs was as follows: a certain amount of CA and urea was thoroughly mixed and then placed into a homemade detonation tube. Next, the tube was evacuated and heated to 358.15 K. Subsequently, different ratios of H2 and O2 were injected into the detonation tube as the explosion source, with the specific H2/O2 molar ratios referring to Table 1 in Section 2.4. Finally, the mixed gases in the detonation tube were ignited to cause a detonation reaction. Under the high temperature and pressure conditions of the detonation reaction, CA and urea were transformed into PDs within 3–5 milliseconds.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the samples were captured using a Tecnai F20 field emission electron microscope (FEI, USA). The samples for TEM tests were prepared as follows: PDs were diluted in anhydrous ethanol, and the suspension was evaporated in air and dripped onto a carbon-coated copper grid. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were characterized by a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) within the range of 1250–3750 cm−1 adopting KBr pressed disks. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were collected using a high-resolution Lambda 750 UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan). The absolute PLQY of PDs was measured employing a Quantaurus-QY instrument (Hamamatsu Photonics Co. Ltd., Japan). The ethanol solution of PDs was diluted to an absorbance of 0.1 for the absolute PLQY test. The pH values were determined using a PB-10 pH meter (Beijing Sartorius Instruments Co. Ltd., China).

Orthogonal experiments were a rapid and efficient experimental design method for studying the effects of multiple factors and levels. To investigate the effects of different factors on the morphology and optical properties of PDs, this paper employed the L9(34) orthogonal table for the design of PDs preparation experiments (Table 1). Based on the characteristics of gaseous detonation, three factors are selected: the total mass of the precursors, the molar ratio of H2 to O2, and the mass ratio of CA to urea. Each factor is set at three levels. The parameters for each experiment are shown in Table 2 , with column D left empty for error analysis. The samples obtained from the experiments are labeled P1-P9.

After the detonation, the products inside the tube are collected, and the photos of the products P1-P9 without any post-processing are shown in Fig. 1 . P1 is yellow-brown with high viscosity. P2 is black-brown with slightly less viscosity than P1. Similarly, P3 is black-brown with further reduced viscosity compared to P2. P4 appears yellow-gray and is almost non-viscous. P5 is gray-white with low viscosity. P6 is light green with a slightly higher viscosity than P5. P7 is yellow and nearly non-viscous. P8 is black-brown with some viscosity. P9 is black-brown with high viscosity. The viscosity of the products is related to the polymer content they contain. A higher polymer content results in greater viscosity. According to Table 2, the mass ratio of CA to urea and the total mass of reactants significantly affect the viscosity of the products. When the mass ratio of CA to urea decreases and the total mass of reactants increases, the viscosity of the products increases.

Fig. 2 reveals that the crystallinity of individual PD in P1 is poor. No carbon lattice structure is observed in the carbon core, indicating the presence of amorphous carbon. Consequently, the reciprocal lattice obtained by the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) displays the electron diffraction pattern of amorphous carbon. The crystallinity of individual PD in P2 improves compared to P1. The incomplete graphite structures are present in the carbon core. The lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the graphene (100) plane [30]. The reciprocal lattice acquired by FFT exhibits a weak single-crystal electron diffraction pattern. Moreover, the crystallinity of individual PD in P3 is high. A relatively complete honeycomb-like graphene structure forms in the carbon core and the lattice spacing is 0.21 nm. The reciprocal lattice gained by FFT demonstrates a complete single-crystal electron diffraction pattern. Similar to P1, the crystallinity of individual PD in P4 is poor, and no carbon lattice structures are observed in the carbon core. The corresponding reciprocal lattice shows the electron diffraction pattern of amorphous carbon. Under electron beam bombardment, the individual PD gradually disappears, further indicating that the interior of PDs in P4 is a polymer. Furthermore, the crystallinity of individual PD in P5 greatly increases compared to P4. The clear graphite carbon strip-like lattices appear in the carbon core and the lattice spacing is 0.21 nm. The reciprocal lattice obtained by FFT displays an incomplete single-crystal electron diffraction pattern. The individual PD of P6 is similar to that of P5, with graphite carbon strip-like lattices in the carbon core. The lattice spacing is also 0.21 nm and the reciprocal lattice acquired by FFT also exhibits an incomplete single-crystal electron diffraction pattern. In addition, the crystallinity of individual PD in P7 is general, with incomplete graphite carbon strip-like lattices in the carbon core. The lattice spacing is 0.21 nm and the reciprocal lattice gained by FFT demonstrates an incomplete single-crystal electron diffraction pattern. The crystallinity of individual PD in P8 is poor and the carbon core is composed of amorphous carbon structures. The reciprocal lattice obtained by FFT shows the electron diffraction pattern of amorphous carbon. Compared to P8, the crystallinity of individual PD in P9 significantly improves. The graphite carbon strip-like lattices appear in the carbon core and the lattice spacing is 0.21 nm. The corresponding electron diffraction pattern exhibits a single-crystal structure. Based on the above observations, the molar ratio of H2 to O2 and the mass ratio of CA to urea have an obvious impact on the crystallinity of PDs. According to Table 2, enhancing the molar ratio of H2 to O2 results in higher crystallinity of the prepared PDs. Conversely, a higher urea content in the reactants leads to lower crystallinity of the prepared PDs. The reason is that an increase in urea causes a larger polymer content in the product, hindering the formation of carbon lattices.

The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 4 ) of P1-P9 exhibit multiple absorption peaks, denoting the presence of different functional groups on the surfaces of all samples. To be more specific, the broad peak at 3200 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of NH. Due to the formation of dimers in P1-P8, the stretching vibration absorption peak of OH in carboxyl groups shifts to lower wavenumbers, resulting in a broad and scattered peak at 2700–3000 cm−1. In contrast, the OH absorption peak of P9 appears at 3400 cm−1. Moreover, the absorption peak at 1670–1700 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CO. For samples with higher degrees of carbonation (such as P3 and P5-P7), the absorption peaks of CO are more pronounced, indicating a higher content of CO. The absorption peaks around 1550 cm−1 and 1450 cm−1 are ascribed to the bending vibration of NH and the presence of CN, respectively. Thus, the initial reaction conditions have almost no effect on the types of surface groups of PDs but significantly affect the proportion of each surface group.

The UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 5 ) of the aqueous solutions of P1-P9 exhibit a broad absorption peak at 330–340 nm corresponding to the n-π* transitions of N- or O-containing groups [31]. This manifests that regardless of the degree of carbonation, the fluorescence does not come from the carbon core states, but may originate from molecular states or surface states. Hence, the initial reaction conditions have little effect on the UV–Vis spectra of PDs and do not alter the fluorescence source of PDs.

As displayed in Fig. 6a, the 10 mg/mL aqueous solutions of P1-P9 appear colorless or light yellow under visible light (i.e., sunlight). And they all emit bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. As exhibited in Fig. 6b, the solid products of P1-P9 without any treatment also emit blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, with P3 possessing the weakest fluorescence intensity, followed by P5.

PL emission spectra of P1-P9 aqueous solutions (Fig. 7 ) exhibit excitation-dependent behavior and the emission wavelength redshifts as the excitation wavelength increases. The maximum excitation/emission wavelengths are 350/434 nm, 350/438 nm, 340/437 nm, 340/431 nm, 340/432 nm, 340/434 nm, 340/434 nm, 360/442 nm, and 360/439 nm, respectively. The emission peaks are broad and extend up to 650 nm. The maximum excitation peaks of P1-P9 range from 340 to 360 nm and the maximum emission peaks range from 430 to 450 nm, displaying blue fluorescence. This is consistent with the properties of most reported blue-light CDs [32–34].

PL emission spectra of P1-P9 solid-state PDs (Fig. 8 ) also demonstrate excitation-dependent behavior, with emission wavelengths increasing as the excitation wavelength improves. The maximum excitation/emission wavelengths are 360/448 nm, 370/452 nm, 370/460 nm, 370/450 nm, 370/459 nm, 370/449 nm, 370/459 nm, 370/462 nm, and 370/448 nm, respectively. Compared to their aqueous solutions, the corresponding maximum excitation/emission wavelengths of P1-P9 solid-state PDs redshift. Additionally, the emission spectrum extends up to 700 nm and covers the entire range of visible light wavelengths. This is beneficial for solid-state PDs acting as white light conversion layers. In conclusion, the initial reaction conditions have a limited effect on the PL emission spectra of both P1-P9 aqueous solution and solid-state PDs, failing to prepare PDs with different fluorescence by adjusting the initial conditions.

To further investigate the PL performance of PDs, the photoexcitation (PLE) and PL emission spectra of different concentrations of P9 aqueous solutions are studied. As displayed in PLE spectra (Fig. 9a)), P9 aqueous solutions with diverse contents exhibit a maximum excitation peak, shifting from 351 to 372 nm with increasing concentration. Specifically, the solid-state PDs exhibit the highest peak intensity. PL emission spectra (Fig. 9b) illustrate that under maximum excitation, the maximum emission peak shifts from 437 to 449 nm as the content of the PDs aqueous solution improves. Similarly, the solid-state PDs display the largest peak intensity. This indicates that the prepared solid-state PDs not only overcome the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching effect but also exhibit aggregation-enhanced emission. Namely, PL intensity improves with the augment of concentration, and the emission peak is redshifted. These properties make them promising for applications in the optical field.

The absolute PLQY of P1-P9 aqueous solutions with an absorbance of 0.1 is measured, and the values are 39.9 %, 32.5 %, 19.1 %, 35.1 %, 23.2 %, 34.8 %, 22.9 %, 35.1 %, and 21.2 %, respectively. This suggests that the initial reaction conditions possess little effect on the UV–Vis spectra and PL emission spectra of PDs, but they significantly influence the PLQY. Using the PLQY of PDs as an indicator, the effects of initial reaction conditions are analyzed through range (R) and variance (Table 5 ). By comparing the values of R, it is obvious that RC > RB > RA. This verifies that the mass ratio of CA to urea has the greatest effect on the PLQY of PDs, followed by the molar ratio of H2 to O2, and finally the total mass of the precursors. The R values of three factors are all greater than those of the error column, indicating that each factor affects the PLQY of PDs.

Fig. 10 provides a clearer understanding of the relationship between PLQY and different factors. Concretely, the mass ratio of CA to urea has the most significant impact on the PLQY of PDs. The maximum PLQY is achieved when the mass ratio of CA to urea is 3:10. As the mass ratio of CA to urea increases, the PLQY obviously decreases. Moreover, the molar ratio of H2 to O2 possesses a significant effect on the PLQY of PDs. The PLQY is highest when the molar ratio of H2 to O2 is 1:1. As the molar ratio of H2 to O2 improves, the PLQY gradually reduces. Additionally, the total mass of the precursors has a relatively evident impact on the PLQY of PDs, with the largest value observed when the precursor mass is at its minimum. Consequently, the optimal preparation conditions for achieving the highest PLQY of PDs are a CA to urea mass ratio of 3:10, an H2 to O2 molar ratio of 1:1, and a total precursor mass of 4 g. To eliminate the influence of errors on the orthogonal experiments, variance analysis results are utilized. According to Table 5, the mass ratio of CA to urea, the molar ratio of H2 to O2, and the total mass of precursor have significant, influential, and certain impacts on the PLQY of PDs, respectively. The order of influence degree is consistent with the consequences of range analysis.

Previous studies have established that the primary factor influencing the PLQY of PDs is the carbonation degree [35]. When the carbonation degree of PDs is low, that is, the polymerization degree of polymers in PDs is high, and the PLQY value is large. The PLQY of the prepared PDs in this study is consistent with previous findings. Combined with the TEM images in section 3.1.2, when the H2 to O2 molar ratio is 2:1, the prepared PDs possess a high degree of carbonation and a low degree of polymerization, resulting in a lower PLQY value, such as P3 and P9. Conversely, when the urea content in the reactants is high, the polymerization degree of the polymers in the prepared PDs is large, leading to a higher PLQY value, such as P1 and P6. These results demonstrate that it is possible to prepare PDs with high PLQY by adjusting the initial reaction conditions, thus laying the groundwork for their application in optoelectronic devices, cell imaging, and other fields.

This study employs orthogonal experiments to investigate the effects of initial reaction conditions on the morphology and optical properties of PDs produced by the gaseous detonation method. The conclusions are as follows:
(1)
The molar ratio of H2 to O2 has the most significant impact on the average particle size of PDs, followed by the mass ratio of CA to urea, and the total mass of precursors.
(2)
The larger the molar ratio of H2 to O2, the higher the crystallinity of the prepared PDs. Conversely, the higher the proportion of urea in the reactants, the lower the crystallinity of PDs. This is because the augment of urea in the reactants causes the increase of polymers in the products, not conducive to the formation of carbon lattices.
(3)
Reducing the mass ratio of CA to urea/molar ratio of H2 to O2/total mass of precursors increases the PLQY of PDs. The mass ratio of CA to urea possesses the greatest effect on the PLQY of PDs, followed by the molar ratio of H2 to O2 and the total mass of precursors.
(4)
The initial conditions have almost no effect on the UV–Vis spectra, PL emission spectra, and the types of surface groups of the prepared PDs. However, changing the initial reaction conditions can alter the content ratio of surface groups.

This research provides a basis for determining the optimal reaction conditions for preparing PDs by the gaseous detonation method. However, due to the rapidity of the detonation reaction, it is difficult to track and characterize the complex intermediate reaction processes. Future research can combine the Z-N-D model of the detonation reaction to reveal the formation mechanism of PDs.

Saiwei Cheng: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Weikai Xu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Chuang He: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Data curation. Shengbo Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. Honghao Yan: Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the Suqian Sci & Tech Program (No. H202313 & K202347), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12172084), the Natural Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province Higher Education Institutions (No. 23KJA560007), and the Jiangsu Civil Architecture Society Project ((2023) No. 4, Item 9).
